task: We’ve got a workflow change coming up at work, and I need a practical plan for switching over without risking bad results. Can you help me lay out a simple way to compare the old process with the new one, test it on a small scale, and decide when it’s safe to fully rely on it?

turns.agent_response:
Absolutely. A practical way to handle a workflow change is to treat it like a controlled process trial, not a full switch on day one.

Here’s a simple, low-risk plan you can use:

## 1) Define the old process as the baseline
Before changing anything, write down:
- What the current process is
- What “good” looks like
- What problems it currently has, if any
- Which output measures matter most  
  - quality
  - speed
  - rework
  - errors
  - customer impact

This gives you something solid to compare against.

## 2) Identify the exact change
Be specific about what is different:
- step added, removed, or reordered
- new tool, form, system, or method
- new handoff or responsibility
- new timing or approval point

If the change is fuzzy, the test will be fuzzy too.

## 3) Run a small pilot
Do not switch everything at once. Start with:
- one shift, one team, one product line, one customer type, or one low-risk batch
- a limited number of cases
- a short time window

Keep the old process available as backup if possible.

## 4) Compare old vs. new with the same checklist
Use the same measures on both versions so the comparison is fair.

Track:
- output quality
- error rate
- time to complete
- rework needed
- missed steps
- any unusual conditions
- operator comments

If possible, have the same person or same type of reviewer check both.

## 5) Change one thing at a time
If problems show up, don’t keep adjusting multiple parts at once.  
Make one change, then test again. That makes it much easier to tell what caused the result.

## 6) Set pass/fail criteria before rollout
Decide in advance what “safe to use” means. For example:
- no increase in defects
- no missed critical steps
- no added safety risk
- equal or better cycle time
- no unresolved issues after the pilot

If the new process meets the criteria, it can move forward. If not, hold it back and fix the weak point.

## 7) Watch for hidden failure points
During the pilot, pay attention to:
- steps people skip under time pressure
- confusion about ownership
- dependence on one experienced person
- differences between shifts or locations
- results that look okay at first but fail later

## 8) Document what happened
Keep a simple log:
- date/time
- who ran it
- which version was used
- what changed
- what result was seen
- any issues or exceptions

This helps prevent guesswork later.

## 9) Expand in stages
If the pilot works:
- move from small pilot to wider use
- keep monitoring for a short period
- confirm results stay stable
- then make it the standard process

## 10) Have a rollback plan
Before full adoption, decide:
- what would trigger a rollback
- who makes that call
- what the fallback process is
- how quickly you can return to the old method

That keeps the change from becoming a single point of failure.

---

## Simple decision rule
You can treat the new workflow as ready for full use when:
- it performs at least as well as the old process
- no critical issues appear in the pilot
- the team can run it consistently
- the results hold up across a small but meaningful sample
- any remaining issues have a clear fix or workaround

If you want, I can turn this into a one-page changeover checklist or a simple pilot test sheet you can use at work.

user_simulator.text:
Yeah, that’s usable. I’d want it a little tighter for the floor, though.

What I need is a version with actual go/no-go criteria, not just “watch for issues.” Something like:
- what counts as acceptable defect rate
- how many samples before calling it stable
- whether the old method stays in parallel or just as backup
- who signs off if the new system starts drifting

And I’d want a simple log sheet format too. Nothing fancy. Just enough to compare old vs. new without people hand-waving it.

turns.agent_response:
Absolutely — here’s a tighter floor-ready version with **clear go/no-go criteria** and a **simple log sheet**.

## 1) Changeover rule
Use the **new workflow in parallel first** if possible.

- **Parallel run** = old method still produces the official result, new method is checked against it.
- If parallel isn’t possible, use the new method on a **small pilot group** while keeping the old method as backup.

**Default recommendation:**  
Run **parallel comparison first**, then switch to new method only after it passes.

---

## 2) Go / No-Go criteria
Set these **before** the pilot starts.

### Go criteria
The new workflow can be approved for full use only if all are true:

- **Defect rate is not worse than the old method**
  - Set a limit like: **no increase in defects**
  - Or, if you need a numeric rule: **new defect rate must stay at or below the old baseline**
- **No critical defects**
  - Any safety, customer, traceability, or spec-critical miss = **No-Go**
- **Stable results across the sample**
  - No pattern of drift, spikes, or repeated rework
- **Operators can run it consistently**
  - No repeated confusion or dependence on one person
- **Any differences are understood and explained**
  - Not just “looks okay”

### No-Go triggers
Stop and hold the change if any of these happen:

- defect rate goes above baseline
- one critical defect is found
- repeated misses in the same step
- results vary too much between people/shifts
- the new method causes extra rework, delay, or confusion
- traceability or revision control is incomplete

---

## 3) Sample size for calling it stable
Keep this simple and practical.

### Minimum pilot
- **At least 10 consecutive samples** with no critical issues
- Better: **20–30 samples** if the process is important or the risk is high

### Stability rule
Call it stable only if:
- results are consistent across the sample
- no critical defects occur
- defect rate stays within the agreed limit
- no unexplained drift shows up

If you see a bad result, **reset the count** after the fix and retest.

---

## 4) Parallel vs backup
### Best option: parallel comparison
Use both methods for a short period:
- old method = baseline
- new method = trial
- compare results side by side

### If parallel is too much
Use the old method as:
- **backup only**
- not the routine check

That means:
- new method runs first
- old method is used only if the new one fails or drifts

---

## 5) Who signs off
Keep the signoff clear so it doesn’t get bounced around.

### Suggested signoff roles
- **Process owner**: final operational call
- **Quality/engineering**: reviews data and confirms pass/fail
- **Supervisor/lead**: confirms floor readiness
- **Operator input**: notes usability issues

### If the new system starts drifting
- **Operator or lead flags it immediately**
- **Supervisor holds the change**
- **Quality/process owner makes the final call** on whether to continue, fix, or roll back

---

## 6) Simple log sheet format
Use one line per sample or batch.

### Log sheet columns
- Date
- Time
- Operator
- Product / job / lot
- Old method result
- New method result
- Match? Y/N
- Defect found? Y/N
- Defect type / note
- Rework needed? Y/N
- Comments
- Hold / release / escalate
- Signoff initials

---

## 7) Simple decision summary
### Approve the new workflow if:
- sample size target is met
- defect rate is at or below baseline
- no critical defects occurred
- results were stable
- process owner + quality approve

### Hold or rollback if:
- any critical defect appears
- defect rate increases
- results drift or vary too much
- traceability or control is unclear
- the team can’t run it consistently

If you want, I can turn this into a **one-page floor checklist** with a **pass/fail box at the bottom** so it’s ready to print.

user_simulator.text:
Yeah, closer. But I’d tighten a couple things before I’d put my name on it.

Need the criteria written so it doesn’t leave room for arguments:

- **Defect rate:** not just “at or below baseline.” Put a hard number on it, or at least say **no increase above the current average over the last X runs**. Otherwise somebody will hand-wave it.
- **Sample size:** 10 is light. For anything that can hit customer quality, I’d want **20 consecutive good samples minimum**, and more if the process changes by shift or product type.
- **Parallel vs backup:** if the new method is still shaky, keep the old one **in parallel for comparison**. If it’s already pretty proven, old method can be backup only. Don’t mix those up.
- **Drift trigger:** define what counts as drift. Example: one critical miss, or **two minor misses in the same checkpoint**, or defect rate over limit on any sample block.
- **Signoff:** make it explicit that **quality owns the stop/go call**, not just the supervisor. Supervisor can flag it, but quality should close it out.

And for the log sheet, I’d want it even plainer:

**Log sheet**
- Date
- Shift
- Operator
- Job / lot
- Old result
- New result
- Match Y/N
- Defect type
- Rework Y/N
- Drift seen Y/N
- Hold / release
- Initials

That’s enough to compare without turning it into paperwork theater.

One more thing: if you want this to hold up on the floor, add a **rollback rule** right on the sheet. Something like: *any critical defect or repeat drift = stop new method and revert to old until reviewed.*

If you want, I can give you a cleaned-up version in a straight checklist format, print-ready.

turns.agent_response:
Absolutely — here’s a tighter, floor-ready version with hard go/no-go language and a plain log sheet.

## Workflow changeover rule
Use the **old method and new method in parallel** first if the new method is still being proven.

- **Parallel** = both methods are run and compared on the same job/lot.
- **Backup only** = old method is only used if the new one fails or drifts.

**Rule of thumb:**
- If the new method is still shaky, keep it **in parallel**.
- If it has already proven itself, old method can be **backup only**.

---

## Go / No-Go criteria

### Go only if all are true:
- **20 consecutive good samples minimum**
- **Defect rate does not exceed the current average over the last X runs**
  - Use your current baseline period, and do **not allow any increase**
- **No critical defects**
- **No repeat drift**
- **No unresolved differences between old and new results**
- **Quality signs off**

### No-Go if any of these happen:
- one critical defect
- defect rate goes above the current average baseline
- **two minor misses in the same checkpoint**
- any drift across a sample block
- results vary too much by shift, operator, or product type
- old and new results do not match and cannot be explained

---

## Drift rule
Treat it as drift if any of these happen:
- **1 critical miss**
- **2 minor misses in the same checkpoint**
- defect rate over limit in any sample block
- repeated mismatch on the same feature

If drift happens:
- **stop the new method**
- **revert to the old method**
- **quality reviews before restart**

---

## Signoff
- **Quality owns the stop / go call**
- Supervisor can flag issues
- Process owner can help decide the fix
- Quality closes it out

If the new system starts drifting, **quality decides whether to hold, fix, or roll back**.

---

## Simple log sheet
Keep it plain:

- Date
- Shift
- Operator
- Job / lot
- Old result
- New result
- Match Y/N
- Defect type
- Rework Y/N
- Drift seen Y/N
- Hold / release
- Initials

---

## Rollback rule
Put this right on the sheet:

**Any critical defect or repeat drift = stop new method and revert to old until reviewed by quality.**

---

If you want, I can turn this into a **one-page print-ready checklist** with a big header, pass/fail lines, and the rollback rule at the bottom.

user_simulator.text:
Yeah, that’s about right. Clean enough to use, and it doesn’t leave much wiggle room.

The hard part is always the baseline. If the current average over the last X runs is garbage, then the whole comparison is garbage too. But as written, this is solid enough for the floor.

The rollback line is the part I’d want bolded. That’s the one people forget when things get busy.

If you’ve got a print-ready one-pager version, that’d be the next thing I’d use.
